meta.description: Severe weather readiness and disaster resources for Arkansas residents.

home.title: Severe Weather Readiness — ARwx
home.description: Essential weather alerts, radar, preparedness tips, and trusted meteorologist sources for Arkansas residents.

hero.heading: Arkansas Severe Weather Readiness
hero.subheading: Essential tools, alerts, and preparedness information for Arkansas residents. Stay informed. Stay safe.

essentials.title: NOAA Weather Radio
essentials.description: 24/7 continuous broadcasts of weather information directly from the National Weather Service. Midland models ($30–$75) are loud enough to wake you during nighttime storms.
bullets: Programmable alerts for your county via SAME codes
Works during power and internet outages
Tip: configure for Tornado Warning only to avoid alert fatigue

links.label: NOAA Weather Radio
links.description: Official NWR station finder and info

links.label: NWS SAME Code Lookup
links.description: Find your county SAME code for programming

essentials.title: WeatherCall
essentials.description: Phone call and text alerts for a precise location — not just your county. Only $10–$12/year.
bullets: Alerts by call and SMS to your phone
Add to your phone's sleep mode exceptions so calls come through at night

links.label: WeatherCall
links.description: Sign up for location-based weather alerts

essentials.title: RadarScope
essentials.description: The go-to professional weather radar app. The only radar app recommended for accurate and timely data.
bullets: Learn to read velocity and reflectivity displays
Available on iOS, Android, and desktop

links.label: NWS Radar (Little Rock)
links.description: Official NWS radar centered on Arkansas

alertsAndApps.heading: Alert Apps & Services
alertsAndApps.description: Supplement your weather radio with these mobile tools for real-time alerts and forecasts. Storms and tornadoes can strike at night — having a system that can wake you is critical.
alertsAndApps.privacyNote: Be cautious with weather apps — many, including well-known ones, monetize by selling user data. Always read the privacy policy before downloading.

links.label: Hello Weather
links.description: Privacy-friendly weather app with a clean interface (no push alerts — use system notifications)

links.label: FEMA App
links.description: Fast mobile alerts, shelter finder, and disaster planning resources

links.label: Red Cross Emergency App
links.description: Set alert locations for family members; available on iOS and Android

links.label: Wireless Emergency Alerts (WEA)
links.description: Free alerts direct to your phone — no app needed. Ensure WEA is enabled in settings.

officialWeather.heading: Official Weather Sources
officialWeather.description: Authoritative forecasts and data from the National Weather Service.

links.label: NWS Little Rock
links.description: Local forecast office for central Arkansas

links.label: Iowa State Mesonet
links.description: Surface observations, radar, and climate data

officialSources.heading: Official Sources & Data
officialSources.description: Authoritative weather data from government and academic sources.

sections.title: Know Your Risk
items: Arkansas is in Tornado Alley — tornado season peaks March through June, with a secondary season in fall
Flash flooding is the #1 weather-related killer in Arkansas
Severe storms can produce tornadoes, damaging winds, and large hail with little warning
Nighttime tornadoes are disproportionately deadly — always have alerts enabled while sleeping

sections.title: Build a Plan
items: Identify your safe room: interior room, lowest floor, away from windows
Practice tornado drills with your household at least twice a year
Know the difference: Watch = conditions favorable; Warning = take shelter now

sections.title: Communication Plan
items: Designate an out-of-town contact as your family's relay point
SMS/text messages are more likely to go through than calls when networks are overloaded
If iMessage fails, switch to SMS — know how to do this on your device
Keep a written list of important phone numbers in case your phone is lost or damaged

sections.title: Build a Kit
items: Water: 1 gallon per person per day for 3 days minimum
Non-perishable food, manual can opener
Flashlight, batteries, NOAA weather radio
First aid kit, medications (7-day supply)
Phone charger (battery bank), important documents in waterproof bag
Emergency ponchos, trash bags, basic toiletries
Cash in small bills, multitool

advancedTips.heading: Advanced Tips
items: Wear sturdy shoes when taking shelter — even at home. Debris and broken glass are common after storms.
Keep helmets accessible for children — bike helmets, batting helmets, anything to protect their heads
Equip each family member with a whistle for emergency signaling if trapped
Save important documents locally on your phone, accessible offline — don't rely on cloud access during outages
Practice drills regularly to reduce panic and ensure everyone knows what to do
Learn to read SPC mesoscale discussions and convective outlooks
Understand radar signatures: hook echoes, debris balls, velocity couplets
Follow local NWS chat discussions during active weather
Consider a storm shelter or safe room if in a manufactured home or tornado-prone area
Review your insurance coverage annually — standard policies often exclude flood damage

meteorologists.heading: Trusted Meteorologists
meteorologists.description: Local meteorologists who provide reliable Arkansas severe weather coverage. Follow for timely updates during active weather.
